original_hypothesis.title: GaugeManager.distributeFees(CL) withdraws 100% CommunityVault token balances each call; any revert or permission mismatch blocks fee distribution for all pools in batch runs
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: For CL pools, GaugeManager._distributeFees reads entire token0/token1 balances from the pool's communityVault and calls IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw(token, balance) for each token, then calls IGaugeCL.claimFees(). If withdraw permissions are missing, communityVault balances are zero, tokens have non-standard behavior, or withdraw reverts for any reason, distributeFees() over pools can fail. Because EpochController.performUpkeep calls distributeFees() (full list), a single problematic pool can disrupt the entire upkeep execution.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Batch execution coupling is a common operational failure mode. Attackers can introduce a problematic pool into pools[] (via permissionless createGauge) that consistently reverts on fee distribution, then rely on automation using the full distributeFees() to repeatedly fail (if ops do not switch to ranged calls).
deployment_assumptions: At least one pool in pools[] is a CL pool where communityVault.withdraw is restricted or can revert under attacker-influenced conditions
Automation/ops commonly use distributeFees() full iteration (via EpochController.performUpkeep)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: EpochController.performUpkeep calls IGaugeManager(gaugeManager).distributeFees() (EpochController.sol ~33-60). No evidence provided that GaugeManager has required vault withdraw permissions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: CL path: communityVault.withdraw(_token0, balanceOf(vault)); communityVault.withdraw(_token1, balanceOf(vault)); IGaugeCL(gauge).claimFees();

preconditions: There exists at least one CL gauge in pools[] (isCLGauge[gauge]==true)
The pool's communityVault.withdraw can revert (permissioned role missing or token issues)
Distribution is executed in batch mode including the problematic pool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All-or-nothing batch upkeep can be bricked by one failing pool
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires ops to use ranged distributeFees(start,finish) to skip failing pools
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after a problematic pool is added

operational_questions: Does GaugeManager have the needed role on Algebra communityVault to withdraw?
Can a pool/communityVault be configured such that withdraw always reverts?
Is EpochController configured to retry with ranges or does it always call full distributions?
validation_requirements: On a fork, attempt distributeFees over mixed pool set including a reverting CL vault
Verify required permissions/roles for withdraw on deployed vault implementation
Assess operational playbooks for skipping failing pools

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create or identify a CL pool whose communityVault.withdraw reverts for GaugeManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: CreateGauge for that pool to add it to pools[]
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for EpochController.performUpkeep to call distributeFees() and observe consistent failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sustain by adding more failing pools if ops adopt ranges

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fee distribution halts or becomes manual/ranged; epoch upkeep reliability reduced
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires pool exclusion procedures; pools cannot be removed from array
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Automated fee routing, epoch flip routines, operational monitoring

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated upkeep failures; distributeFees reverting at same pool index
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: No fees reaching internal bribes for CL pools despite trading volume
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Newly created CL gauges shortly before failures start

validation_result.mechanism: Unprotected external calls in batch loop cause all-or-nothing failure
validation_result.impact_summary: A single reverting CL pool's communityVault.withdraw() blocks the entire EpochController.performUpkeep transaction, preventing both distributeAll() and distributeFees() from completing for all pools.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a structural code defect in GaugeManager._distributeFees() for CL pools. The function makes raw external calls to IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw() without any try/catch error handling. When called via the batch distributeFees() from EpochController.performUpkeep(), a single reverting pool reverts the entire transaction, including the distributeAll() call that precedes it. The vault withdraw can revert due to: (1) missing COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role on GaugeManager (the code defines the role constant but never uses it to grant access), (2) zero-amount withdrawals if the vault implementation rejects them, or (3) any token-level issue. Since GaugeManager.createGauge() is largely permissionless (requires only whitelisted tokens with a connector), the pools array grows without bound, and there's no mechanism to remove pools.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence clearly shows: (1) EpochController.performUpkeep calls distributeFees() after distributeAll() in a single atomic transaction with no try/catch. (2) GaugeManager.distributeFees() iterates all pools in a simple for loop with no error handling. (3) _distributeFees() for CL pools calls IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw() directly without protection. (4) There's no mechanism to remove pools from the pools[] array. The ranged distributeFees(uint256,uint256) variant exists but performUpkeep does not use it.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Raw external calls to IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw() without try/catch. If the vault reverts (missing permissions, zero amounts, token issues), the entire batch transaction fails. The communityVault is an external Algebra contract with its own access control that may not include GaugeManager as an authorized withdrawer.

performUpkeep calls distributeAll() and distributeFees() sequentially in a single transaction. If distributeFees() reverts, the entire transaction including distributeAll() is rolled back. This couples emission distribution with fee distribution, meaning a fee-related failure blocks emissions too.
Iterates ALL pools without error handling. No try/catch around _distributeFees calls. A single failing pool reverts the entire loop and the caller transaction.

calling_sequence: 1. Chainlink Automation or EPOCH_MANAGER calls performUpkeep()
4. distributeFees() loops through all pools[i], calling _distributeFees(pools[i])
5. For a CL pool, _distributeFees reads communityVault balances and calls IAlgebraCommunityVault(communityVault).withdraw(token, balance)
6. If withdraw() reverts (permissions, zero amount, token issue), _distributeFees reverts
7. distributeFees() reverts, causing performUpkeep() to revert entirely
8. Both distributeAll() and distributeFees() effects are rolled back
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is performUpkeep -> distributeFees -> _distributeFees -> IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw(). The withdraw call at GaugeManager.sol line ~197 is unprotected. The Algebra CommunityVault withdraw function requires the caller to have COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role (defined as bytes32 constant in GaugeManager but never used to grant the role). Even if permissions are correctly configured externally, calling withdraw with _balanceToken0 == 0 could also revert depending on vault implementation. The gauge's claimFees() at line ~200 is another unprotected external call that can revert if the internal_bribe is misconfigured.

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one pool in pools[] is a CL pool where communityVault.withdraw is restricted or can revert
Automation/ops commonly use distributeFees() full iteration (via EpochController.performUpkeep)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show: (1) flip-epoch.js calls performUpkeep('0x') confirming automation uses the full batch path. (2) No script grants COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role to GaugeManager on any vault. (3) GaugeManager defines COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER_ROLE constant but never uses it. (4) set-chainlink.js configures automation registries, confirming Chainlink automation triggers performUpkeep. Scripts are SILENT on vault permission configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is feasible. An attacker can: (1) Identify or create a CL pool with whitelisted tokens where communityVault.withdraw will revert for GaugeManager (e.g., if vault requires COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role that GaugeManager lacks). (2) Call createGauge for that pool to add it to pools[]. Since createGauge is largely permissionless (only requires whitelisted/connector tokens), this is achievable. (3) Once added, every subsequent call to distributeFees() will revert when it reaches this pool. (4) EpochController.performUpkeep will consistently fail, blocking both emission distribution and fee distribution. Even without an attacker, if ANY CL pool's vault configuration changes or has permission issues, the same failure occurs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. The defect can halt the automated epoch upkeep process entirely. Since performUpkeep atomically calls both distributeAll() and distributeFees(), a fee distribution failure prevents emission distribution as well. Recovery requires: (1) ops to identify the failing pool, (2) switch to manual ranged calls using distributeFees(start, finish) and distribute(start, finish) to skip the failing pool, or (3) fix vault permissions externally. Pools cannot be removed from the pools[] array, so the problematic pool persists permanently. The lack of try/catch means even transient failures (e.g., zero-balance vaults that revert on zero withdrawals) can block the system.
recommended_mitigations: Wrap _distributeFees() calls in try/catch blocks within distributeFees() to isolate failures per pool
Use try/catch around IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw() calls within _distributeFees()
Add a balance check before calling withdraw: skip if balance is 0
Decouple distributeAll() and distributeFees() in performUpkeep so that a fee distribution failure does not block emission distribution
Add a mechanism to remove or disable pools from the pools[] array
Verify and document that GaugeManager has COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role on all Algebra vaults before creating CL gauges
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock Algebra pool and CommunityVault where withdraw() requires a specific role. 2. Deploy GaugeManager and EpochController with standard configuration. 3. Create a CL gauge for the mock pool (ensure tokens are whitelisted). 4. Do NOT grant COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role to GaugeManager on the vault. 5. Call performUpkeep and observe the revert. 6. Alternatively, create a vault where withdraw(token, 0) reverts, and ensure the vault has zero balance to trigger the zero-amount revert path.
uncertainties: The exact implementation of IAlgebraCommunityVault.withdraw() is not in scope - whether it reverts on zero amounts or lacks specific access control depends on the Algebra deployment
Whether the Algebra vault's withdraw function requires COMMUNITY_FEE_WITHDRAWER role or has more permissive access control in the deployed version
Whether the protocol team has configured vault permissions off-chain through mechanisms not captured in the deployment scripts
additional_testing_needed: Fork test against deployed Algebra vault to verify if GaugeManager can call withdraw()
Test distributeFees() with a mix of CL and non-CL pools where one CL pool's vault revert behavior varies
Test what happens when communityVault balance is exactly 0 for both tokens
Verify whether IGaugeCL.claimFees() can independently revert (e.g., internal_bribe misconfiguration)
